Apple___Apple_scab.description: Apple scab is a common fungal disease caused by Venturia inaequalis. It produces olive-green to brown lesions on leaves and fruit, leading to premature defoliation and unmarketable fruit.
causes: Fungal pathogen Venturia inaequalis
Cool, wet spring weather (10–24°C)
Prolonged leaf wetness periods
Infected fallen leaves from previous season
symptoms: Olive-green to dark brown spots on leaves
Scabby, cracked lesions on fruit
Premature leaf drop
Deformed fruit in severe cases
treatment: Apply fungicides (Captan, Myclobutanil, or Mancozeb) at 7–10 day intervals during wet periods
Use preventive copper-based sprays before bud break
Remove and destroy infected plant debris
Apply lime sulfur sprays during dormancy
prevention: Plant scab-resistant apple varieties (Liberty, Enterprise, Redfree)
Rake and destroy fallen leaves in autumn
Ensure good air circulation by proper pruning
Avoid overhead irrigation; use drip systems
Apply dormant oil sprays in late winter
Apple___Apple_scab.watering_advice: Water at the base of the tree using drip irrigation. Avoid wetting foliage. Water early morning so leaves dry by afternoon. Reduce watering frequency during cool, wet periods.
Apple___Apple_scab.fertilizer_recommendation: Apply balanced NPK fertilizer (10-10-10) in early spring. Avoid excessive nitrogen which promotes lush, susceptible growth. Add potassium for improved disease resistance.

severity_tips.mild: Monitor closely and apply preventive fungicide. Remove affected leaves.
severity_tips.moderate: Initiate fungicide spray program immediately. Prune for better airflow.
severity_tips.severe: Aggressive fungicide program required. Consider removing heavily infected branches. Consult local extension service.

Apple___Black_rot.description: Black rot is a fungal disease caused by Botryosphaeria obtusa affecting apples. It causes leaf spots, fruit rot, and cankers on branches, leading to significant crop loss.
causes: Fungal pathogen Botryosphaeria obtusa
Warm, humid weather (24–29°C)
Wounds from insects, hail, or pruning
Stressed or weak trees
symptoms: Purple spots on leaves with yellow halos
Dark, sunken lesions on fruit
Mummified fruit remaining on tree
Reddish-brown cankers on branches
treatment: Apply captan or thiophanate-methyl fungicides
Prune out infected branches 6–8 inches below visible infection
Remove mummified fruit from trees and ground
Use copper-based fungicides during early season
prevention: Maintain tree vigor through proper nutrition
Control fire blight and other wounds
Remove all mummified fruit and dead wood
Apply dormant pruning to improve air circulation
Manage insect pests to reduce entry wounds
Apple___Black_rot.watering_advice: Use drip irrigation to minimize leaf wetness. Water deeply but infrequently to maintain soil moisture without waterlogging. Ensure proper drainage around the root zone.
Apple___Black_rot.fertilizer_recommendation: Apply nitrogen in spring to promote healthy growth. Supplement with calcium to improve fruit integrity. Avoid over-fertilization which increases disease susceptibility.

severity_tips.mild: Remove affected plant parts and apply preventive fungicide.
severity_tips.moderate: Fungicide program with captan or thiophanate-methyl. Prune affected branches.
severity_tips.severe: Aggressive pruning of infected wood. Multiple fungicide applications. Consider tree removal if heavily infected.

Apple___Cedar_apple_rust.description: Cedar apple rust is caused by Gymnosporangium juniperi-virginianae, requiring both apple and cedar/juniper hosts to complete its life cycle. It causes bright orange-yellow spots on apple leaves and fruit.
causes: Fungal pathogen Gymnosporangium juniperi-virginianae
Requires cedar or juniper trees nearby as alternate host
Warm, wet spring conditions
Wind-dispersed spores from cedar galls
symptoms: Bright orange-yellow spots on upper leaf surfaces
Orange tube-like structures on leaf undersides
Deformed and spotted fruit
Premature defoliation
treatment: Apply myclobutanil or propiconazole fungicides from pink bud stage
Spray every 7–10 days during wet weather
Remove nearby cedar/juniper galls in late winter
prevention: Plant resistant apple varieties (Redfree, Liberty, Freedom)
Remove cedar and juniper trees within 300 meters if possible
Apply preventive fungicides starting at bud break
Improve air circulation through pruning
Apple___Cedar_apple_rust.watering_advice: Avoid overhead watering. Use drip irrigation or soaker hoses. Water in the morning to allow quick drying of any accidentally wet foliage.
Apple___Cedar_apple_rust.fertilizer_recommendation: Balanced fertilization with NPK 10-10-10 in spring. Potassium supplementation improves resistance. Avoid excessive nitrogen.

severity_tips.mild: Apply fungicide preventively and remove nearby juniper galls.
severity_tips.moderate: Intensive fungicide schedule. Remove affected leaves.
severity_tips.severe: Multi-spray fungicide program. Evaluate removal of nearby cedar hosts.

Apple___healthy.description: Your apple plant appears healthy! Healthy apple trees show vibrant green leaves, strong branches, and no signs of disease or pest damage. Maintaining this health requires proper care.
symptoms: No disease symptoms detected
Vibrant green foliage
Normal leaf texture and color
prevention: Continue regular monitoring for early disease detection
Maintain proper pruning schedule for air circulation
Apply preventive dormant sprays annually
Monitor for common pests (codling moth, aphids)
Apple___healthy.watering_advice: Deep watering 2–3 times per week during dry periods. Apply mulch around base to retain moisture. Ensure excellent drainage to prevent root rot. Reduce watering in autumn.
Apple___healthy.fertilizer_recommendation: Apply balanced slow-release fertilizer (10-10-10) in early spring when buds begin to swell. Supplement with compost annually. Avoid fertilizing after midsummer to prevent tender growth before winter.

severity_tips.mild: Plant is healthy. Continue current care routine.
severity_tips.moderate: N/A
severity_tips.severe: N/A

Blueberry___healthy.description: Your blueberry plant is healthy! Healthy blueberries display vibrant green to dark green foliage, strong canes, and no signs of disease or stress. Blueberries thrive in acidic, well-drained soils.
symptoms: No disease symptoms detected
Healthy green foliage
Normal cane development
prevention: Maintain soil pH between 4.5–5.5 with sulfur amendments if needed
Mulch with pine bark or wood chips to retain acidity and moisture
Monitor for mummy berry, botrytis blight, and stem canker
Net plants to protect from birds during ripening
Blueberry___healthy.watering_advice: Blueberries have shallow roots and need consistent moisture. Provide 1–2 inches of water per week. Mulch heavily to retain soil moisture. Avoid waterlogging — blueberries are sensitive to root rot.
Blueberry___healthy.fertilizer_recommendation: Use acid-forming fertilizers (ammonium sulfate or formulations for blueberries/azaleas). Apply in early spring and again after harvest. Avoid lime and high-pH fertilizers. Iron chelate corrects yellowing on alkaline soils.

severity_tips.mild: Plant is healthy. Continue current care routine.
severity_tips.moderate: N/A
severity_tips.severe: N/A

Cherry_(including_sour)___Powdery_mildew.description: Powdery mildew on cherry is caused by Podosphaera clandestina. It forms white powdery fungal growth on young leaves, shoots, and fruit, leading to distortion, reduced photosynthesis, and poor fruit development.
causes: Fungal pathogen Podosphaera clandestina
Warm, dry days with cool, humid nights (18–28°C)
Poor air circulation within the canopy
Excessive nitrogen promoting lush, susceptible growth
Overwintering spores on infected buds and bark
symptoms: White to grayish powdery coating on leaf surfaces
Curling and distortion of young leaves
Stunted new shoot growth
Russeting or scarring on fruit surface
Premature defoliation in severe infections
treatment: Apply sulfur-based fungicides or potassium bicarbonate at first sign of infection
Use systemic fungicides (myclobutanil, trifloxystrobin) for established infections
Apply neem oil as an organic control option
Remove and destroy severely infected shoots and leaves
prevention: Plant resistant cherry varieties where available
Prune to open the canopy and improve air circulation
Avoid excess nitrogen fertilization
Apply preventive sulfur sprays starting at bud break
Remove infected shoot tips during the growing season
Cherry_(including_sour)___Powdery_mildew.watering_advice: Use drip irrigation to keep foliage dry. Avoid wetting leaves — powdery mildew is unusual in that it thrives in humid air but is inhibited by free moisture on leaves. Water at the base of the tree in the morning.
Cherry_(including_sour)___Powdery_mildew.fertilizer_recommendation: Balanced NPK fertilization. Limit nitrogen to avoid producing excessively soft, susceptible young growth. Potassium improves cell wall strength. Calcium applications help harden tissue against infection.

severity_tips.mild: Apply sulfur or potassium bicarbonate spray. Remove affected shoot tips.
severity_tips.moderate: Switch to systemic fungicide. Prune heavily to open canopy. Repeat spray every 7–10 days.
severity_tips.severe: Aggressive systemic fungicide program. Heavy canopy removal. Significant crop quality impact likely — focus on protecting next season's buds.

Cherry_(including_sour)___healthy.description: Your cherry tree is healthy! Healthy cherry trees display glossy, dark green leaves, strong branch structure, and no signs of disease or pest damage. Good orchard hygiene keeps cherry trees productive.
symptoms: No disease symptoms detected
Glossy green foliage
Normal shoot development
prevention: Apply dormant copper sprays each winter to prevent bacterial canker and brown rot
Monitor for brown rot, leaf spot, and powdery mildew during the growing season
Prune in dry weather to reduce infection through cuts
Net trees during fruit ripening to deter birds which spread brown rot
Cherry_(including_sour)___healthy.watering_advice: Deep watering weekly during dry periods. Cherries dislike waterlogged soil. Mulch around base but keep mulch away from the trunk. Reduce irrigation as fruit ripens to prevent splitting.
Cherry_(including_sour)___healthy.fertilizer_recommendation: Apply balanced fertilizer in early spring. Established trees need moderate nitrogen. Potassium for fruit quality and disease resistance. Avoid late-season nitrogen which delays hardening before winter.

severity_tips.mild: Plant is healthy. Continue current care routine.
severity_tips.moderate: N/A
severity_tips.severe: N/A

Corn_(maize)___Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray.description: Gray leaf spot, caused by Cercospora zeae-maydis, is one of the most significant foliar diseases of corn worldwide. It produces distinctive rectangular lesions running parallel to leaf veins and can cause severe yield loss under favorable conditions.
causes: Fungal pathogen Cercospora zeae-maydis
Warm temperatures (25–30°C) with high humidity
Extended periods of dew or leaf wetness
Reduced tillage systems that leave infected crop residue
Dense plant populations reducing air circulation
symptoms: Small, rectangular to elongated gray to tan lesions with parallel sides
Lesions bordered by leaf veins giving a rectangular appearance
Lesions may coalesce causing large areas of blighted tissue
Severe blighting of upper leaves reduces photosynthesis
Stalk rot may develop in severely affected plants
treatment: Apply strobilurin fungicides (azoxystrobin, pyraclostrobin) at tasseling for best results
Use triazole fungicides (propiconazole, tebuconazole) as alternatives
Apply at VT (tasseling) to R2 (blister) growth stage for economic benefit
Scout fields regularly to determine if economic threshold is reached
prevention: Plant resistant or tolerant hybrid varieties — the most effective management tool
Rotate corn with non-host crops (soybeans, small grains) for at least one year
Till infected residue to accelerate decomposition
Reduce plant population density to improve air movement
Avoid fields with history of severe gray leaf spot in high-risk years
Corn_(maize)___Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray.watering_advice: Avoid overhead irrigation as it extends the leaf wetness period favorable for disease. Use subsurface drip or furrow irrigation. Irrigate in the morning. Adequate water management is critical — both drought stress and excessive moisture increase susceptibility.
Corn_(maize)___Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray.fertilizer_recommendation: Adequate balanced nutrition for optimal plant health and resistance. Balanced nitrogen application — deficiency increases stress susceptibility. Potassium improves stalk strength and overall disease resistance. Silicon applications have shown some benefit in reducing severity.

severity_tips.mild: Scout fields. If infection is below ear level at tasseling, fungicide may not be economical.
severity_tips.moderate: Apply fungicide at tasseling. Monitor upper canopy. Yield loss is likely without treatment.
severity_tips.severe: Fungicide application essential. Significant yield loss possible. Prioritize resistant hybrids for next season's planting.

Corn_(maize)___Common_rust_.description: Common rust of corn is caused by Puccinia sorghi. It produces small, powdery, cinnamon-brown pustules on both leaf surfaces and can reduce photosynthesis and yield significantly.
causes: Fungal pathogen Puccinia sorghi
Cool temperatures (16–23°C) with high humidity
Wind-dispersed spores from other infected plants
Extended periods of leaf wetness
symptoms: Small, circular to oval, cinnamon-brown pustules on leaves
Pustules rupture to release powdery orange-red spores
Heavy infection causes yellowing and death of leaves
Pustules present on both upper and lower leaf surfaces
treatment: Apply triazole fungicides (propiconazole, tebuconazole) at early infection signs
Apply fungicide at tasseling stage for maximum protection
Strobilurin fungicides also effective
prevention: Plant resistant hybrid varieties
Plant early to avoid peak rust season
Monitor fields regularly during cool, wet periods
Crop rotation reduces initial inoculum
Corn_(maize)___Common_rust_.watering_advice: Avoid overhead irrigation when possible. Irrigate in the morning to allow foliage to dry during the day. Maintain adequate soil moisture for plant health but avoid creating conditions that favor rust development.
Corn_(maize)___Common_rust_.fertilizer_recommendation: Adequate nitrogen for optimal plant health. Potassium improves disease resistance. Avoid over-application of nitrogen which makes plants more susceptible. Silicon applications may improve resistance.

severity_tips.mild: Monitor crop. Apply fungicide if rust appears before tasseling.
severity_tips.moderate: Apply fungicide immediately. Focus on upper canopy protection.
severity_tips.severe: Aggressive fungicide application. Yield loss likely — protect remaining healthy tissue. Assess economic threshold for treatment.

Corn_(maize)___Northern_Leaf_Blight.description: Northern leaf blight, caused by Exserohilum turcicum (formerly Helminthosporium turcicum), is a major foliar disease of corn. It produces large, cigar-shaped lesions and can cause yield losses of 30–50% in severe cases.
causes: Fungal pathogen Exserohilum turcicum
Moderate temperatures (18–27°C) with extended periods of high humidity
Infected corn residue left on the soil surface
Reduced tillage systems that preserve infected residue
Wind-dispersed spores during humid weather
symptoms: Long, elliptical, cigar-shaped lesions 2.5–15 cm in length
Lesions are tan to grayish-green, often with wavy or irregular margins
Dark sporulation (olive-gray) visible on lesion surface in humid conditions
Lesions may coalesce causing large areas of dead tissue
Severe blighting reduces ear fill and grain quality
treatment: Apply foliar fungicides (azoxystrobin, propiconazole, tebuconazole) at early tassel stage
Fungicide applications most effective when initiated at first appearance of lesions
Strobilurin + triazole premix products offer broad-spectrum control
prevention: Select resistant or tolerant hybrid varieties — most effective strategy
Rotate with non-host crops such as soybeans for at least one season
Till infected residue to reduce inoculum levels
Scout fields regularly from V8 (8-leaf) stage onward
Avoid fields with heavy residue history of northern leaf blight
Corn_(maize)___Northern_Leaf_Blight.watering_advice: Minimize overhead irrigation as extended leaf wetness promotes sporulation and infection. Use furrow or drip systems. Irrigate early in the day. Avoid creating humidity pockets with excessive plant density.
Corn_(maize)___Northern_Leaf_Blight.fertilizer_recommendation: Balanced nutrition to maintain healthy, vigorous plants. Adequate nitrogen without excess — high nitrogen can increase susceptibility. Potassium is important for overall plant health and disease tolerance. Correct any micronutrient deficiencies identified by soil testing.

severity_tips.mild: Monitor regularly. If lesions are below the ear leaf before tasseling, consider fungicide application.
severity_tips.moderate: Apply fungicide at or before tasseling. Yield loss is expected without treatment.
severity_tips.severe: Immediate fungicide application. Significant grain yield and quality reductions are expected. Plan for resistant hybrids next season.

Corn_(maize)___healthy.description: Your corn plant appears healthy! Healthy corn shows bright green leaves, strong stalks, and vigorous growth. Proper management will ensure a productive harvest.
symptoms: No disease symptoms
Green, upright leaves
Normal growth and development
prevention: Scout fields regularly for pests and diseases
Maintain proper plant spacing for air circulation
Rotate with non-grass crops to break disease cycles
Use certified disease-free seed
Corn_(maize)___healthy.watering_advice: Corn requires 1–1.5 inches of water per week. Critical periods are germination, tasseling, and silking. Use furrow or drip irrigation. Avoid overhead watering during tasseling which can wash pollen.
Corn_(maize)___healthy.fertilizer_recommendation: Heavy feeder. Apply nitrogen in split applications: at planting and side-dress at knee-high stage. Phosphorus at planting for root development. Potassium for stalk strength. Soil test for zinc which is often deficient.

severity_tips.mild: Plant is healthy. Continue current management practices.
severity_tips.moderate: N/A
severity_tips.severe: N/A

Grape___Black_rot.description: Grape black rot, caused by Guignardia bidwellii, is a devastating fungal disease affecting all green parts of the vine. Infected berries shrivel into hard black mummies.
causes: Fungal pathogen Guignardia bidwellii
Warm, wet weather (21–27°C)
Overwintering infected mummified berries
Infected canes and tendrils
symptoms: Small brown spots with dark borders on leaves
Black, shriveled mummified berries
Dark lesions on young shoots and tendrils
Fruit losses can reach 100% in severe cases
treatment: Apply mancozeb, myclobutanil, or captan fungicides
Begin spraying at shoot emergence (2–4 inches)
Continue on 7–14 day schedule through fruit set
Remove all mummified berries from vineyard
prevention: Remove all mummified fruit and infected canes during dormant pruning
Ensure good air circulation through canopy management
Use drip irrigation instead of overhead sprinklers
Apply dormant copper spray before growth begins
Grape___Black_rot.watering_advice: Use drip irrigation exclusively. Avoid wetting the canopy. Water early in the day. Ensure excellent drainage. Reduce watering during cool, wet periods to prevent waterlogging.
Grape___Black_rot.fertilizer_recommendation: Balanced vine nutrition with emphasis on potassium for fruit quality and disease resistance. Avoid excessive nitrogen which promotes soft, susceptible growth. Soil pH should be 6.0–6.5.

severity_tips.mild: Apply fungicide. Remove infected berries and leaves.
severity_tips.moderate: Intensive fungicide program. Remove all infected material.
severity_tips.severe: Complete canopy removal of infected wood. Aggressive fungicide schedule. Risk of total crop loss — prioritize protecting remaining healthy clusters.

Grape___Esca_(Black_Measles).description: Esca, also known as Black Measles or Grapevine Apoplexy, is a complex vascular disease caused by a consortium of wood-rotting fungi including Phaeomoniella chlamydospora and Phaeoacremonium species. It is one of the most economically damaging grapevine trunk diseases globally.
causes: Fungal complex including Phaeomoniella chlamydospora, Phaeoacremonium minimum, and Fomitiporia mediterranea
Pruning wounds serve as primary infection courts
Warm, dry summers following wet winters
Infected planting material
Old vines are more susceptible than young vines
symptoms: Interveinal leaf chlorosis and necrosis creating a tiger-stripe or mosaic pattern
Dark, measle-like spots on berry skin
Sudden wilting and death of entire shoots or vines (apoplectic form)
Internal wood shows brown streaking and white rot in cross-section
Stunted shoot growth and reduced vigor
treatment: No effective curative chemical treatment exists once vines are infected
Surgically remove infected wood by cutting back to healthy tissue
Apply wound sealants (Topsin paste, paraffin) immediately after pruning
In severe cases, trunk renewal by training a new sucker to replace the infected trunk
Sodium arsenite (historical use — now banned in most countries)
prevention: Use certified disease-free planting material
Protect all pruning wounds immediately with wound sealants or fungicide paste
Prune during dry weather to minimize infection risk
Delay pruning until late in the dormant season to reduce wound susceptibility
Remove and burn infected wood — never leave in the vineyard
Grape___Esca_(Black_Measles).watering_advice: Maintain consistent soil moisture to reduce vine stress, which exacerbates Esca symptom expression. Avoid prolonged drought stress during summer. Drip irrigation is preferred. Waterlogging should also be avoided as it weakens root systems.
Grape___Esca_(Black_Measles).fertilizer_recommendation: Maintain balanced vine nutrition to reduce plant stress. Potassium for canopy health. Avoid excessive nitrogen. Biostimulants and silicon applications may help reduce overall vine stress. Boron and zinc support wood and shoot health.

severity_tips.mild: Mark affected vines. Avoid stressing them. Apply wound sealant to all pruning cuts.
severity_tips.moderate: Consider surgical wood removal to healthy tissue. Apply wound protectants. Monitor closely.
severity_tips.severe: Trunk renewal or vine removal may be necessary. Infected vines are a permanent inoculum source for the vineyard.

Grape___Leaf_blight_(Isariopsis_Leaf_Spo.description: Grape leaf blight caused by Pseudocercospora vitis (formerly Isariopsis clavispora) produces angular leaf spots that can lead to early defoliation, reducing photosynthetic capacity and weakening vines going into winter.
causes: Fungal pathogen Pseudocercospora vitis
Warm, humid conditions late in the growing season
Dense canopy with poor air circulation
Prolonged leaf wetness from rain or overhead irrigation
Infected leaf debris from previous seasons
symptoms: Angular, dark brown to reddish-brown leaf spots bounded by leaf veins
Yellow halos may surround lesions on upper leaf surface
Dark olive-gray sporulation on the underside of lesions
Premature defoliation as lesions coalesce
Reduced vine vigor and winter hardiness from early leaf loss
treatment: Apply copper-based fungicides or mancozeb at first sign of symptoms
Myclobutanil and other DMI fungicides provide good control
Apply every 10–14 days during warm, wet periods
Remove and destroy fallen infected leaves
prevention: Prune and train vines for maximum canopy airflow
Apply copper dormant sprays before bud break
Remove and compost or burn infected leaf litter after harvest
Avoid overhead irrigation — use drip systems
Scout vineyards from mid-summer onward when disease typically appears
Grape___Leaf_blight_(Isariopsis_Leaf_Spo.watering_advice: Drip irrigation is essential. Avoid any wetting of the leaf canopy. Water early in the day if overhead irrigation is unavoidable. Ensure good soil drainage — waterlogged conditions weaken vines and increase susceptibility.
Grape___Leaf_blight_(Isariopsis_Leaf_Spo.fertilizer_recommendation: Balanced vine nutrition throughout the growing season. Avoid late-season nitrogen applications which delay vine hardening. Potassium supports canopy health and improves disease tolerance. Foliar micronutrients can help maintain vigor in high-production vineyards.

severity_tips.mild: Apply copper or mancozeb fungicide. Improve canopy management.
severity_tips.moderate: Fungicide spray program every 10–14 days. Remove infected leaves. Thin canopy.
severity_tips.severe: Aggressive fungicide program. Significant defoliation will impact vine vigor and next season's productivity. Focus on protecting remaining leaves.

Grape___healthy.description: Your grapevine appears healthy! Healthy grapevines show strong canes, vibrant green leaves, and no signs of disease or pest damage. Proper vineyard management ensures continued health.
symptoms: No disease symptoms detected
Vibrant green foliage
Strong cane growth
prevention: Annual dormant pruning to maintain open canopy
Monitor for powdery mildew, black rot, and downy mildew
Apply preventive dormant copper sprays
Control leafhoppers, grape berry moth, and Japanese beetles
Grape___healthy.watering_advice: Deep watering weekly during growing season. Established vines are drought-tolerant but benefit from irrigation during dry spells. Reduce water significantly as harvest approaches to concentrate flavors.
Grape___healthy.fertilizer_recommendation: Apply balanced fertilizer in early spring. Nitrogen in spring for vine vigor. Potassium for fruit quality. Avoid over-fertilization which leads to excessive vegetative growth at the expense of fruit.

severity_tips.mild: Plant is healthy. Continue current vineyard management.
severity_tips.moderate: N/A
severity_tips.severe: N/A

Orange___Haunglongbing_(Citrus_greening).description: Huanglongbing (HLB), also called Citrus Greening, is the most destructive citrus disease in the world. Caused by the bacterium Candidatus Liberibacter asiaticus and spread by the Asian citrus psyllid, there is currently no cure. Infected trees decline and die within years.
causes: Bacterial pathogen Candidatus Liberibacter asiaticus (CLas)
Transmitted by Asian citrus psyllid (Diaphorina citri)
Spread through infected budwood during propagation
No soil transmission — insect vector is required
symptoms: Asymmetric (blotchy) mottling of leaves — distinguishes HLB from nutrient deficiency
Yellowing of shoots (huanglongbing means 'yellow dragon disease' in Chinese)
Small, lopsided, bitter fruit that fails to color normally
Premature fruit drop
Progressive tree decline and eventual death within 5–8 years
treatment: No cure currently exists — infected trees cannot be saved
Remove and destroy infected trees immediately to prevent psyllid spread
Thermotherapy (heat treatment) shows limited experimental promise
Intensive nutritional programs may extend productivity of mildly affected trees
Research into antimicrobial compounds is ongoing
prevention: Control Asian citrus psyllid populations with systemic insecticides (imidacloprid, thiamethoxam)
Use certified HLB-free nursery stock only
Inspect all new trees before planting
Establish psyllid monitoring programs in groves
Support regional HLB management and reporting programs
Quarantine and destroy infected trees promptly
Orange___Haunglongbing_(Citrus_greening).watering_advice: Maintain optimal tree health through consistent irrigation to slow disease progression in mildly affected trees. Avoid drought stress. Drip or microjet irrigation is preferred. Well-nourished, well-watered trees tolerate symptoms longer.
Orange___Haunglongbing_(Citrus_greening).fertilizer_recommendation: Intensive nutritional management extends the productive life of mildly infected trees. Foliar applications of micronutrients (zinc, manganese, boron) and macronutrients are recommended. Trunk injection of antibiotics (oxytetracycline) is approved in some regions to temporarily suppress bacterial populations.

severity_tips.mild: Tag and monitor tree closely. Intensify psyllid control. Report to local agricultural authority.
severity_tips.moderate: Tree removal is strongly recommended to prevent spread. Psyllid management in surrounding trees is critical.
severity_tips.severe: Remove and destroy tree immediately. Do not move plant material off-site. Notify local citrus authorities — HLB is a regulated disease in many regions.

Peach___Bacterial_spot.description: Bacterial spot, caused by Xanthomonas arboricola pv. pruni, is one of the most serious diseases of peaches and nectarines. It affects leaves, fruit, and twigs, leading to defoliation, reduced yield, and unmarketable fruit.
causes: Bacterial pathogen Xanthomonas arboricola pv. pruni
Warm, rainy, and windy weather (24–30°C)
Rain splash and wind spread bacteria from infected tissue
Overwintering bacteria in infected twigs
Susceptible varieties in humid climates
symptoms: Small, water-soaked spots on leaves that turn purple-brown with yellow margins
Holes in leaves ('shothole' appearance) as necrotic tissue falls out
Sunken, dark, cracked lesions on fruit
Twig cankers and dieback
Premature defoliation reducing fruit size and quality
treatment: Apply copper-based bactericides during wet periods
Oxytetracycline sprays during bloom and early fruit development
Remove and destroy infected twigs during dormant pruning
Spray on 5–7 day intervals during high-risk periods
prevention: Plant resistant or tolerant varieties (Contender, Redhaven, Reliance)
Apply copper dormant sprays before bud swell
Prune to improve air circulation through canopy
Avoid sites with high winds that spread bacteria
Do not plant in low areas with poor air drainage
Peach___Bacterial_spot.watering_advice: Use drip irrigation to keep leaves dry. Avoid any overhead irrigation. Bacteria spread rapidly on wet leaves during windy conditions. Water at the base of the tree early in the morning.
Peach___Bacterial_spot.fertilizer_recommendation: Moderate, balanced fertilization. Avoid excessive nitrogen which promotes succulent growth highly susceptible to infection. Potassium improves tissue resistance. Calcium applications strengthen fruit skin, reducing infection sites.

severity_tips.mild: Apply copper bactericide. Remove affected leaves and twigs. Improve air circulation.
severity_tips.moderate: Intensive copper and oxytetracycline spray program. Prune affected branches. Fruit may be unmarketable.
severity_tips.severe: Significant defoliation and fruit loss likely. Consider replacing with resistant varieties for future seasons. Aggressive dormant copper program essential.

Peach___healthy.description: Your peach tree is healthy! Healthy peach trees display glossy, lanceolate green leaves, strong scaffold branches, and no signs of disease or pest damage. Peaches require specific care for continued health.
symptoms: No disease symptoms detected
Glossy green lance-shaped leaves
Strong branch structure
prevention: Apply copper dormant sprays annually before bud swell
Monitor for peach leaf curl, bacterial spot, and brown rot
Thin fruit to 15–20 cm spacing to improve size and reduce disease load
Monitor for peach tree borers and oriental fruit moth
Peach___healthy.watering_advice: Deep watering weekly during dry periods. Peaches are sensitive to both drought and waterlogging. Provide 1–2 inches per week. Mulch to conserve moisture. Reduce watering as fruit ripens to concentrate sugars.
Peach___healthy.fertilizer_recommendation: Apply balanced fertilizer in early spring. Nitrogen for shoot growth. Potassium for fruit quality. Avoid late-season nitrogen. Zinc deficiency is common in peaches — supplement if leaf symptoms appear.

severity_tips.mild: Plant is healthy. Continue current care routine.
severity_tips.moderate: N/A
severity_tips.severe: N/A

Pepper,_bell___Bacterial_spot.description: Bacterial spot of pepper is caused by Xanthomonas euvesicatoria. It is a major disease of bell and chili peppers in warm, humid climates, causing leaf lesions, defoliation, and fruit blemishes that reduce marketability.
causes: Bacterial pathogen Xanthomonas euvesicatoria
Warm temperatures (24–30°C) with frequent rainfall
Wind-driven rain spreads bacteria through the canopy
Infected seed is a primary introduction source
Overwintering bacteria on infected plant debris
symptoms: Small, water-soaked spots on leaves that become brown with yellow halos
Irregular, dark, raised lesions on fruit with a scabby appearance
Shothole appearance on leaves as necrotic centers fall out
Premature defoliation exposing fruit to sunscald
Twig dieback in severe infections
treatment: Apply copper-based bactericides (copper hydroxide, copper sulfate) every 5–7 days
Tank-mix copper with mancozeb for improved efficacy
Remove and destroy severely infected plants and leaves
Apply acibenzolar-S-methyl (SAR activator) as a preventive tool
prevention: Use certified pathogen-free or hot-water-treated seed
Plant resistant varieties where available
Rotate peppers with non-solanaceous crops for 2–3 years
Avoid overhead irrigation — use drip systems
Avoid working in the field when plants are wet
Sanitize transplanting equipment between uses
Pepper,_bell___Bacterial_spot.watering_advice: Drip irrigation is strongly recommended. Bacteria spread explosively on wet foliage. Never use overhead irrigation. Water at the base of plants in the morning. Mulch to reduce soil splash onto lower leaves.
Pepper,_bell___Bacterial_spot.fertilizer_recommendation: Balanced fertilization at transplanting and again at first fruit set. Avoid excessive nitrogen. Calcium applications strengthen cell walls and reduce infection sites. Potassium improves overall disease resistance.

severity_tips.mild: Apply copper bactericide. Remove affected leaves. Switch to drip irrigation immediately.
severity_tips.moderate: Intensive spray program every 5 days. Defoliation may expose fruit to sunscald — apply shade cloth if needed.
severity_tips.severe: Consider removing and destroying heavily infected plants to protect neighboring ones. Fruit is likely unmarketable. Plan for resistant varieties and improved sanitation next season.

Pepper,_bell___healthy.description: Your bell pepper plant is healthy! Healthy peppers show bright green, glossy foliage, strong upright stems, and prolific flowering. Peppers thrive in warm conditions with consistent care.
symptoms: No disease symptoms detected
Bright glossy green foliage
Upright healthy growth
prevention: Monitor for bacterial spot, phytophthora blight, and anthracnose
Apply preventive copper sprays during wet weather
Support plants with stakes or cages to prevent breakage
Control aphids and thrips which can transmit viruses
Pepper,_bell___healthy.watering_advice: Consistent moisture is critical for good fruit set and preventing blossom end rot. Provide 1–2 inches per week using drip irrigation. Avoid drought stress which causes flower and fruit drop. Mulch heavily to retain moisture.
Pepper,_bell___healthy.fertilizer_recommendation: Balanced fertilizer at transplanting. High phosphorus promotes root development and flowering. Side-dress with nitrogen when first fruits begin to set. Calcium prevents blossom end rot. Avoid excessive nitrogen which promotes foliage over fruit.

severity_tips.mild: Plant is healthy. Continue current care routine.
severity_tips.moderate: N/A
severity_tips.severe: N/A

Potato___Early_blight.description: Early blight of potato is caused by Alternaria solani. It produces characteristic target-spot lesions on leaves and can cause significant defoliation and yield reduction.
causes: Fungal pathogen Alternaria solani
Warm temperatures (24–29°C) alternating with cool nights
Plant stress from drought or nutrient deficiency
Infected plant debris in soil
symptoms: Brown to black lesions with concentric rings (target pattern)
Yellow tissue surrounding lesions
Premature defoliation starting with older leaves
Dark, sunken lesions on tubers at harvest
treatment: Apply chlorothalonil, mancozeb, or copper-based fungicides
Apply every 7–10 days during warm, humid weather
Ensure good coverage of lower leaves where infection begins
prevention: Use certified disease-free seed potatoes
Rotate crops — avoid potatoes after tomatoes or peppers
Maintain adequate soil fertility to reduce plant stress
Apply mulch to prevent soil splash
Hill potatoes properly to protect tubers
Potato___Early_blight.watering_advice: Use drip irrigation or furrow irrigation. Avoid wetting foliage. Water in the morning. Maintain consistent soil moisture to prevent drought stress which increases susceptibility. Adequate moisture during tuber initiation is critical.
Potato___Early_blight.fertilizer_recommendation: Adequate nitrogen for good plant growth without excess. Potassium is critical for tuber development and disease resistance. Balanced fertilization reduces plant stress susceptibility. Apply according to soil test results.

severity_tips.mild: Apply preventive fungicide. Remove lower infected leaves.
severity_tips.moderate: Fungicide program every 7–10 days. Improve air circulation.
severity_tips.severe: Aggressive fungicide schedule every 5–7 days. Assess if early vine kill would protect tubers from tuber infection.

Potato___Late_blight.description: Potato late blight, caused by Phytophthora infestans, is the most destructive potato disease. The same pathogen caused the Irish Potato Famine of the 1840s. It can destroy entire fields within days under favorable conditions.
causes: Oomycete pathogen Phytophthora infestans
Cool, moist conditions (10–20°C) with extended leaf wetness
Infected tubers or volunteer plants
Windborne spores from neighboring infected plants
symptoms: Water-soaked, pale green to dark brown lesions rapidly expanding
White sporulation on leaf undersides in humid conditions
Entire leaves and stems turn brown and collapse
Tubers develop firm, reddish-brown internal rot
treatment: Apply copper-based fungicides or chlorothalonil preventively
Use systemic fungicides (metalaxyl, mefenoxam, fluopicolide) when late blight is present
Apply every 5–7 days during high-risk periods
Consider vine killing to protect tubers if disease is severe
prevention: Plant certified late blight-free seed potatoes
Use resistant varieties (Defender, Jacqueline Lee, Elba)
Monitor Blitecast or similar disease forecasting models
Ensure excellent air circulation
Destroy volunteer potato plants and infected cull piles
Potato___Late_blight.watering_advice: Use drip irrigation exclusively. Never irrigate in the evening. Avoid creating prolonged leaf wetness. Ensure excellent field drainage. During high-risk periods, minimize any additional moisture.
Potato___Late_blight.fertilizer_recommendation: Balanced nutrition to maintain plant vigor. Avoid excessive nitrogen. Phosphorus and potassium for tuber development and disease resistance. Calcium applications improve tuber quality and storage.

severity_tips.mild: Apply copper fungicide immediately. Monitor daily. Alert neighbors.
severity_tips.moderate: Apply systemic fungicide. Consider defoliating (vine killing) to protect tubers.
severity_tips.severe: Emergency vine killing. Apply fungicide to soil to protect tubers. Late blight moves extremely fast — act within hours or entire crop may be lost.

Potato___healthy.description: Your potato plant appears healthy! Healthy potato plants show dark green leaves, strong stems, and vigorous growth. Proper management will ensure excellent tuber development.
symptoms: No disease symptoms
Dark green, healthy foliage
Vigorous growth
prevention: Scout fields regularly for late blight and early blight
Monitor for Colorado potato beetle and aphids
Apply preventive fungicides during high-risk periods
Hill plants to protect developing tubers
Potato___healthy.watering_advice: Consistent moisture is critical, especially during tuber initiation and bulking. Provide 1–2 inches per week. Use drip or furrow irrigation. Irregular watering causes knobby or hollow tubers. Reduce water 2 weeks before harvest.
Potato___healthy.fertilizer_recommendation: Apply nitrogen at planting and side-dress when plants are 6 inches tall. High potassium requirement for tuber development. Phosphorus for root and tuber growth. Boron and sulfur supplementation improves quality. Soil pH 4.8–5.4 preferred.

severity_tips.mild: Plant is healthy. Continue current care practices.
severity_tips.moderate: N/A
severity_tips.severe: N/A

Raspberry___healthy.description: Your raspberry plant is healthy! Healthy raspberries display lush green leaves, strong primocanes and floricanes, and vigorous growth. Raspberries require specific management for sustained productivity.
symptoms: No disease symptoms detected
Lush green foliage
Vigorous cane growth
prevention: Prune out old floricanes after fruiting to reduce disease pressure
Monitor for raspberry cane blight, spur blight, and anthracnose
Ensure good air circulation with a trellis system
Apply preventive copper or lime sulfur sprays in early spring
Raspberry___healthy.watering_advice: Consistent moisture needed especially during fruit development. Provide 1–1.5 inches per week via drip irrigation. Avoid overhead watering which promotes fungal diseases. Mulch heavily with straw or wood chips to retain moisture.
Raspberry___healthy.fertilizer_recommendation: Apply balanced fertilizer in early spring. Nitrogen promotes vigorous cane growth. Potassium for fruit quality. Avoid fall nitrogen applications which reduce winter hardiness. Soil pH 5.5–6.5 is optimal for raspberries.

severity_tips.mild: Plant is healthy. Continue current care routine.
severity_tips.moderate: N/A
severity_tips.severe: N/A

Soybean___healthy.description: Your soybean plant is healthy! Healthy soybeans display rich trifoliate green leaves, strong stems, and good nodulation from nitrogen-fixing bacteria. Soybeans are important legume crops requiring specific management.
symptoms: No disease symptoms detected
Healthy trifoliate leaves
Good nodule development on roots
prevention: Monitor for sudden death syndrome, white mold, and soybean cyst nematode
Scout fields regularly for Asian soybean rust in high-risk areas
Use certified seed with appropriate seed treatments
Rotate with non-legume crops to break disease and pest cycles
Soybean___healthy.watering_advice: Soybeans are relatively drought tolerant but critical water periods are pod fill (R3–R6 stages). Provide 1–1.5 inches per week during pod fill. Well-drained soils are essential — soybeans are very sensitive to waterlogging which kills beneficial root nodules.
Soybean___healthy.fertilizer_recommendation: Soybeans fix their own nitrogen through rhizobium nodules — avoid excess nitrogen fertilization which inhibits nodulation. Phosphorus and potassium are important. Inoculate seed with Bradyrhizobium japonicum if planting in new fields. Sulfur is often deficient and important for protein synthesis.

severity_tips.mild: Plant is healthy. Continue current management practices.
severity_tips.moderate: N/A
severity_tips.severe: N/A

Squash___Powdery_mildew.description: Powdery mildew of squash and cucurbits is caused primarily by Podosphaera xanthii (formerly Sphaerotheca fuliginea). It is one of the most common and widespread cucurbit diseases, forming white powdery colonies on leaf surfaces.
causes: Fungal pathogen Podosphaera xanthii
Warm, dry days (20–27°C) with moderate humidity
Unlike most fungal diseases, does NOT require free moisture on leaves
Dense plant canopies with poor air circulation
Airborne conidia spread rapidly over long distances
symptoms: White, powdery fungal colonies initially on older leaf surfaces
Colonies spread to cover entire leaves, petioles, and stems
Infected leaves become yellow then brown and die prematurely
Reduced fruit size and quality from early defoliation
Fruit may show poor coloring or premature ripening
treatment: Apply sulfur-based fungicides as soon as white colonies appear
Potassium bicarbonate sprays are effective organic options
Systemic fungicides (myclobutanil, trifloxystrobin) control established infections
Neem oil or plant-extract based products for organic production
Remove and destroy severely infected leaves to reduce spore load
prevention: Plant resistant squash varieties (many modern varieties have PM resistance)
Space plants widely for maximum air circulation
Avoid excessive nitrogen which promotes succulent, susceptible leaves
Apply preventive sulfur sprays before disease appears
Apply compost tea as a biologic preventive measure
Squash___Powdery_mildew.watering_advice: Unlike most fungal diseases, powdery mildew is not favored by wet leaves — overhead irrigation may actually temporarily reduce spore load. However, drip irrigation is still preferred for overall plant health. Maintain consistent moisture to reduce drought stress which increases susceptibility.
Squash___Powdery_mildew.fertilizer_recommendation: Balanced fertilization. Avoid excessive nitrogen which creates soft, rapidly expanding leaf tissue highly susceptible to powdery mildew. Potassium and silica applications improve leaf surface resistance. Silicon (potassium silicate foliar sprays) has shown significant suppression of powdery mildew in research.

severity_tips.mild: Apply sulfur or potassium bicarbonate. Remove affected older leaves.
severity_tips.moderate: Switch to systemic fungicide. Defoliate severely infected leaves. Improve plant spacing.
severity_tips.severe: Aggressive fungicide program. Significant yield and quality reduction expected. Prioritize resistant varieties next season.

Strawberry___Leaf_scorch.description: Strawberry leaf scorch is caused by the fungus Diplocarpon earlianum. It is one of the most common foliar diseases of strawberries, causing purple to brown spots on leaves that may coalesce into large blighted areas.
causes: Fungal pathogen Diplocarpon earlianum
Cool to moderate temperatures (15–25°C) with prolonged leaf wetness
Overhead irrigation and rain splash spread spores
Dense plant canopies with poor air movement
Infected plant crowns and leaves from previous season
symptoms: Small, irregular, dark purple to brown spots on upper leaf surfaces
Spots lack the distinct purple border of other strawberry diseases
Lesions may coalesce causing large irregular blighted areas
Leaf margins appear scorched giving the disease its name
Severely infected leaves turn reddish and die prematurely
treatment: Apply myclobutanil, captan, or copper-based fungicides
Begin applications in early spring at green tip stage
Continue on 10–14 day intervals through the season
Remove and destroy severely infected old leaves during renovation
prevention: Plant disease-resistant strawberry varieties
Use certified disease-free planting material
Renovate beds after harvest by mowing, thinning, and fertilizing
Use drip irrigation to keep foliage dry
Remove old leaves and plant debris in autumn
Avoid planting in low areas with poor air drainage
Strawberry___Leaf_scorch.watering_advice: Drip irrigation is strongly recommended. Overhead irrigation creates the prolonged leaf wetness that promotes disease. Water early in the morning if overhead watering is unavoidable. Mulch with straw to reduce soil splash onto leaves.
Strawberry___Leaf_scorch.fertilizer_recommendation: Balanced fertilization after harvest renovation. Avoid excessive nitrogen which promotes lush, susceptible growth. Potassium improves disease tolerance. Boron is important for strawberry fruit set and plant health. Soil pH 5.5–6.5 is optimal.

severity_tips.mild: Apply fungicide. Remove affected leaves. Switch to drip irrigation.
severity_tips.moderate: Fungicide program every 10–14 days. Renovate bed by removing old foliage.
severity_tips.severe: Aggressive fungicide schedule. Consider full renovation or replanting with resistant varieties if disease recurs annually.

Strawberry___healthy.description: Your strawberry plant is healthy! Healthy strawberries display bright green, trifoliate leaves, strong crowns, and good runner production. Proper bed management ensures continued productivity.
symptoms: No disease symptoms detected
Bright green trifoliate leaves
Strong crown and runner development
prevention: Monitor for gray mold (botrytis), leaf scorch, and angular leaf spot
Renovate beds immediately after harvest
Remove old and diseased leaves regularly
Apply straw mulch to protect fruit from soil contact and splash
Strawberry___healthy.watering_advice: Consistent moisture is critical for fruit development. Provide 1–2 inches per week via drip irrigation. Critical period is during fruit set and development. Mulch with straw to retain moisture and keep fruit clean.
Strawberry___healthy.fertilizer_recommendation: Apply balanced fertilizer after renovation and again in early spring the following year. Avoid high nitrogen during fruiting which reduces fruit quality. Potassium improves fruit flavor and disease resistance. Boron is important for fruit set.

severity_tips.mild: Plant is healthy. Continue current care routine.
severity_tips.moderate: N/A
severity_tips.severe: N/A

Tomato___Bacterial_spot.description: Bacterial spot of tomato is caused by Xanthomonas species (primarily X. vesicatoria). It is a serious disease in warm, humid climates causing leaf spots, defoliation, and fruit blemishes that reduce marketability.
causes: Bacterial pathogen Xanthomonas vesicatoria and related species
Warm temperatures (25–30°C) with frequent rain or overhead irrigation
Wind and rain spread bacteria through the canopy
Infected seed is a primary introduction route
Infected plant debris from previous season
symptoms: Small, water-soaked spots on leaves that become dark brown with yellow halos
Spots may have greasy appearance when held to light
Shothole appearance as necrotic tissue falls from lesions
Dark, raised, scab-like lesions on green fruit
Premature defoliation exposing fruit to sunscald
treatment: Apply copper bactericides (copper hydroxide, copper sulfate) every 5–7 days during wet weather
Tank-mix copper with mancozeb for improved residual activity
Apply acibenzolar-S-methyl (Actigard) as an SAR (systemic acquired resistance) activator
Remove and destroy infected plant material
prevention: Use certified pathogen-free or hot-water-treated seed (50°C for 25 minutes)
Plant resistant varieties where available
Rotate tomatoes with non-solanaceous crops for 2–3 years
Use drip irrigation — never overhead irrigation
Sanitize stakes, cages, and tools between uses
Avoid working in the field when plants are wet
Tomato___Bacterial_spot.watering_advice: Drip irrigation is essential. Bacteria spread explosively on wet foliage during windy conditions. Never use overhead sprinklers on tomatoes. Mulch around plants to prevent soil splash onto lower leaves. Water early in the morning.
Tomato___Bacterial_spot.fertilizer_recommendation: Balanced fertilizer at transplanting. Avoid excessive nitrogen. Calcium applications improve cell wall strength and reduce infection sites. Potassium improves disease resistance and fruit quality.

severity_tips.mild: Apply copper bactericide. Remove infected leaves. Ensure drip irrigation is used.
severity_tips.moderate: Intensive spray program every 5 days. Defoliation may expose fruit to sunscald.
severity_tips.severe: Consider removing and destroying heavily infected plants. Fruit crop is likely unmarketable. Plan for resistant varieties and crop rotation next season.

Tomato___Early_blight.description: Early blight is caused by Alternaria solani and is one of the most common tomato diseases. It affects leaves, stems, and fruit, causing concentric ring patterns resembling a target.
causes: Fungal pathogen Alternaria solani
Warm temperatures (24–29°C) with high humidity
Infected plant debris in soil
Splashing water spreading spores
symptoms: Dark brown spots with concentric rings (target pattern)
Yellow halo around lesions
Premature leaf drop from bottom up
Dark lesions on stems and fruit
treatment: Apply chlorothalonil, mancozeb, or copper fungicides
Remove and destroy infected leaves immediately
Spray every 7–10 days during wet conditions
Use biofungicide Bacillus subtilis as organic option
prevention: Rotate tomatoes with non-solanaceous crops every 3 years
Use disease-free transplants
Mulch around plants to prevent soil splash
Avoid working with plants when wet
Space plants adequately for air circulation
Tomato___Early_blight.watering_advice: Water at soil level using drip irrigation or soaker hoses. Never wet foliage. Water in the morning. Maintain consistent moisture to prevent stress. Mulch to retain moisture and reduce splash.
Tomato___Early_blight.fertilizer_recommendation: Balanced fertilizer at transplanting. Side-dress with nitrogen when first flowers appear. Calcium supplementation reduces fruit problems. Avoid excessive nitrogen which promotes disease-susceptible growth.

severity_tips.mild: Remove lower infected leaves. Apply preventive fungicide.
severity_tips.moderate: Fungicide spray program. Remove all infected material. Improve air circulation.
severity_tips.severe: Aggressive fungicide application every 5–7 days. Consider removing severely infected plants to protect others.

Tomato___Late_blight.description: Late blight, caused by Phytophthora infestans, is the infamous disease that caused the Irish Potato Famine. It can devastate tomato crops rapidly under cool, wet conditions.
causes: Oomycete pathogen Phytophthora infestans
Cool temperatures (10–20°C) with high humidity
Prolonged leaf wetness
Infected potato or tomato volunteers nearby
symptoms: Water-soaked, pale green to brown lesions on leaves
White fuzzy growth on leaf undersides
Dark brown, greasy-looking spots that spread rapidly
Fruit shows firm, brown, greasy lesions
treatment: Apply copper-based fungicides or chlorothalonil immediately
Use systemic fungicides (metalaxyl, mefenoxam) for systemic control
Remove and destroy all infected plant tissue
Apply fungicide preventively in high-risk weather
prevention: Plant resistant varieties (Mountain Magic, Defiant, Plum Regal)
Monitor weather forecasts and apply preventive fungicides before rain
Remove volunteer potatoes and tomatoes nearby
Ensure excellent air circulation
Avoid overhead irrigation
Tomato___Late_blight.watering_advice: Strictly use drip irrigation only. Never wet foliage under any circumstances. Water early morning. Reduce watering during cool, wet periods. Ensure excellent soil drainage.
Tomato___Late_blight.fertilizer_recommendation: Avoid excessive nitrogen. Focus on phosphorus and potassium for root development and disease resistance. Apply calcium supplements to improve plant cell wall integrity against pathogens.

severity_tips.mild: Immediately apply copper fungicide. Remove affected leaves. Alert neighboring gardeners.
severity_tips.moderate: Aggressive fungicide program. Consider removing entire infected branches.
severity_tips.severe: Remove entire infected plants. Apply systemic fungicide to remaining plants. Late blight spreads extremely fast — act within hours.

Tomato___Leaf_Mold.description: Tomato leaf mold is caused by Passalora fulva (formerly Fulvia fulva / Cladosporium fulvum). It is primarily a problem in greenhouse tomatoes and humid climates, causing distinctive olive-green to gray mold on leaf undersides.
causes: Fungal pathogen Passalora fulva
High humidity (above 85%) and moderate temperatures (22–25°C)
Poor greenhouse ventilation
Dense plant canopy preventing air movement
Spores spread by air currents, workers, and contaminated tools
symptoms: Pale green to yellow spots on upper leaf surfaces
Olive-green to gray-brown velvety mold growth on leaf undersides directly below spots
Infected leaves curl upward, turn yellow, and drop
Severe infections cause significant defoliation
Rarely affects fruit, but blossoms may be infected
treatment: Apply chlorothalonil, mancozeb, or copper-based fungicides
Use systemic fungicides (myclobutanil, difenoconazole) for established infections
Improve greenhouse ventilation to reduce humidity
Remove and destroy infected leaves promptly
prevention: Plant resistant tomato varieties (most modern greenhouse varieties have Cf resistance genes)
Maintain greenhouse relative humidity below 85% through ventilation and heating
Space plants to maximize air circulation
Remove lower leaves to improve airflow
Sterilize greenhouse between crops
Use drip irrigation to keep foliage dry
Tomato___Leaf_Mold.watering_advice: Drip irrigation is essential. Avoid any wetting of foliage. In greenhouses, water early in the day and ensure good ventilation to allow humidity to drop before evening. Avoid cold water on warm plants.
Tomato___Leaf_Mold.fertilizer_recommendation: Balanced fertilization to maintain plant vigor. Calcium is important for leaf tissue integrity. Avoid excessive nitrogen which promotes dense, humid canopies. Potassium improves overall disease resistance.

severity_tips.mild: Improve ventilation. Remove infected leaves. Apply preventive fungicide.
severity_tips.moderate: Fungicide program every 7–10 days. Aggressively remove infected foliage. Reduce humidity.
severity_tips.severe: Consider removing heavily infected plants in greenhouse settings to protect others. Sterilize growing area before replanting.

Tomato___Septoria_leaf_spot.description: Septoria leaf spot, caused by Septoria lycopersici, is one of the most destructive diseases of tomato foliage. It spreads rapidly from the bottom of the plant upward and can cause complete defoliation under favorable conditions.
causes: Fungal pathogen Septoria lycopersici
Warm, wet weather (20–25°C)
Rain splash and overhead irrigation spread spores
Infected plant debris in the soil
Prolonged periods of leaf wetness
symptoms: Numerous small (3–5 mm), circular spots with dark brown margins and light gray centers
Tiny black fruiting bodies (pycnidia) visible in the center of spots with a hand lens
Spots appear first on older lower leaves and progresses upward
Severe yellowing and death of infected leaves
Rapid defoliation can expose fruit to sunscald
treatment: Apply chlorothalonil, mancozeb, or copper-based fungicides every 7–10 days
Begin at first sign of disease or preventively in wet weather
Remove and destroy infected lower leaves to slow upward spread
Bacillus subtilis products are organic alternatives
prevention: Rotate tomatoes with non-solanaceous crops for 2–3 years
Remove all infected crop debris at end of season
Mulch soil surface to prevent rain splash of spores
Use drip irrigation — never overhead
Space plants adequately for air circulation
Stake or cage plants to keep foliage off the ground
Tomato___Septoria_leaf_spot.watering_advice: Drip or soaker hose irrigation is essential. The disease spreads by water splash. Keep foliage as dry as possible. Water in the morning. Mulch around plants with straw or plastic to prevent soil-to-leaf splash.
Tomato___Septoria_leaf_spot.fertilizer_recommendation: Balanced fertilization to maintain plant vigor. Avoid excessive nitrogen which promotes lush, dense canopy. Potassium improves disease tolerance. Calcium strengthens leaf tissue. Maintain consistent fertility throughout the growing season.

severity_tips.mild: Remove lower infected leaves. Apply fungicide. Mulch soil. Switch to drip irrigation.
severity_tips.moderate: Aggressive fungicide program every 7 days. Remove all infected leaves. Defoliation is likely without treatment.
severity_tips.severe: Entire plant may defoliate. Apply fungicide to protect remaining upper canopy and fruit. Plant losses and fruit sunscald are likely.

Tomato___Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider.description: Two-spotted spider mites (Tetranychus urticae) are not insects but arachnids. They are one of the most economically important pests of tomatoes and many other crops, thriving in hot, dry conditions and causing stippled, bronzed foliage.
causes: Pest: Tetranychus urticae (Two-spotted spider mite)
Hot, dry weather (above 28°C with low humidity) triggers population explosions
Overuse of broad-spectrum insecticides that kill natural predators
Dust on plant surfaces which interferes with natural mite predators
Water-stressed plants are highly susceptible
symptoms: Fine stippling (tiny yellow or white dots) on upper leaf surfaces from feeding punctures
Leaves turn bronze, yellow, or silver as infestation progresses
Fine silk webbing on undersides of leaves and between branches in heavy infestations
Premature leaf drop and severe defoliation
Microscopic reddish-brown mites visible on leaf undersides with a hand lens
treatment: Apply miticides (abamectin, bifenazate, spiromesifen) — rotate modes of action to prevent resistance
Insecticidal soap or neem oil for organic control — thorough coverage of leaf undersides is essential
Release predatory mites (Phytoseiulus persimilis, Neoseiulus californicus) for biological control
Strong water sprays to knock mites off plants can reduce populations temporarily
prevention: Maintain good plant irrigation to prevent drought stress
Avoid broad-spectrum insecticides that kill predatory mites and other beneficial insects
Control dust around fields by irrigating dirt roads and borders
Introduce predatory mites preventively in greenhouse settings
Monitor plants regularly, especially on leaf undersides, during hot dry weather
Tomato___Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider.watering_advice: Maintain consistent, adequate soil moisture — water-stressed plants are far more susceptible to severe mite damage. Drip irrigation is best. Occasional overhead watering in hot weather can reduce mite populations but is not a substitute for miticides in established infestations.
Tomato___Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider.fertilizer_recommendation: Well-nourished plants tolerate mite feeding better. Avoid excessive nitrogen which makes leaf tissue more attractive to mites. Potassium and silicon applications may reduce plant susceptibility. Do not over-fertilize — overly lush plants can support larger mite populations.

severity_tips.mild: Apply insecticidal soap or neem oil. Ensure adequate irrigation. Introduce predatory mites.
severity_tips.moderate: Apply miticide. Rotate to a different mode of action on second application. Remove severely affected leaves.
severity_tips.severe: Aggressive miticide rotation program. Significant yield loss and defoliation expected. Consider releasing predatory mites after chemical treatment to prevent resurgence.

Tomato___Target_Spot.description: Target spot of tomato is caused by Corynespora cassiicola. It produces concentric ring lesions on leaves, stems, and fruit similar to early blight but is a distinct disease, increasingly prevalent in warm, humid growing regions.
causes: Fungal pathogen Corynespora cassiicola
Warm, humid conditions (25–32°C) with high relative humidity
Extended periods of leaf wetness
Dense plant canopy with poor air circulation
Infected crop debris and soilborne inoculum
symptoms: Circular to irregular brown lesions with concentric rings (target pattern) on leaves
Lesions often have a yellow halo, similar to early blight
Dark brown, sunken lesions on fruit — a key distinguishing feature
Defoliation starting from lower leaves
Stem lesions at petiole attachments
treatment: Apply chlorothalonil, azoxystrobin, or fluxapyroxad fungicides
Begin applications preventively at first sign of disease
Spray every 7–14 days during wet conditions
Remove and destroy infected leaves to reduce spore load
prevention: Rotate with non-solanaceous crops
Use drip irrigation and avoid wetting foliage
Ensure adequate plant spacing for air movement
Remove and destroy crop debris at season end
Use transplants from disease-free sources
Tomato___Target_Spot.watering_advice: Drip irrigation is essential — prolonged leaf wetness is the primary environmental driver of this disease. Keep foliage dry. Water in the morning. Mulch around plant bases to prevent soil splash.
Tomato___Target_Spot.fertilizer_recommendation: Balanced nutrition to maintain plant vigor. Calcium reduces fruit infection susceptibility. Avoid excessive nitrogen. Potassium for disease tolerance and fruit quality. Consistent fertility reduces stress-related susceptibility.

severity_tips.mild: Apply fungicide. Remove infected lower leaves. Improve ventilation.
severity_tips.moderate: Fungicide program every 7–10 days. Significant defoliation risk without treatment.
severity_tips.severe: Aggressive fungicide schedule. Both foliage and fruit are at risk. Fruit lesions make crop unmarketable.

Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.description: Tomato Yellow Leaf Curl Virus (TYLCV) is a devastating begomovirus transmitted exclusively by the silverleaf whitefly (Bemisia tabaci). It causes severe stunting and yield loss, and there is no cure for infected plants.
causes: Viral pathogen Tomato Yellow Leaf Curl Virus (TYLCV)
Transmitted persistently by silverleaf whitefly (Bemisia tabaci biotype B)
A single viruliferous whitefly can infect plants within minutes of feeding
Whitefly populations build rapidly in warm conditions
Infected transplants can introduce virus to new areas
symptoms: Upward and inward curling of young leaves
Yellowing (chlorosis) of leaf margins and interveinal areas
Small, crumpled leaves with a leathery texture
Severe stunting of the entire plant
Dramatically reduced or complete absence of fruit set when infected young
treatment: No cure exists for virus-infected plants — remove and destroy infected plants immediately
Control whitefly populations with insecticides (imidacloprid, thiamethoxam, pyriproxyfen)
Insecticidal soaps and neem oil for organic whitefly management
Use reflective mulches to deter whitefly landing
Install insect-proof nets (50-mesh) in greenhouse production
prevention: Plant TYLCV-resistant or tolerant tomato varieties — most effective strategy
Use certified virus-free transplants from reputable sources
Control whitefly populations before they reach economic thresholds
Use yellow sticky traps for monitoring and mass trapping
Apply reflective aluminum mulch at planting to repel whiteflies
Remove and destroy infected plants promptly to prevent spread
Establish a whitefly-free nursery stage with insect-proof screens
Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.watering_advice: Maintain optimal plant health through consistent irrigation — healthy plants tolerate virus stress slightly better than stressed ones. Drip irrigation is preferred. Avoid drought stress which compounds virus-related stunting.
Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.fertilizer_recommendation: Maintain good plant nutrition to sustain as much productivity as possible from mildly affected plants. Balanced NPK with micronutrients. Avoid excessive nitrogen which can attract higher whitefly populations. Potassium for overall plant health and stress tolerance.

severity_tips.mild: Remove infected plants immediately. Intensify whitefly control. Alert neighboring growers.
severity_tips.moderate: Remove all infected plants. Apply whitefly insecticide to remaining crop. Screen greenhouse openings.
severity_tips.severe: Entire crop may need to be destroyed to prevent whitefly build-up and spread. TYLCV can devastate entire regions — report to local agricultural authority if an outbreak occurs.

Tomato___Tomato_mosaic_virus.description: Tomato Mosaic Virus (ToMV) is a highly contagious plant virus that causes mosaic-like patterns on leaves and can significantly reduce yield and fruit quality.
causes: Tobacco Mosaic Virus (TMV) or Tomato Mosaic Virus
Transmitted through infected hands, tools, or contaminated soil
Insect vectors (aphids) in some cases
Infected seed or transplants
symptoms: Mosaic or mottled light/dark green pattern on leaves
Leaf curling and distortion
Stunted plant growth
Reduced and malformed fruit
treatment: No cure exists — infected plants must be removed
Isolate and destroy infected plants immediately
Sterilize tools with bleach solution (1:9 ratio)
Wash hands thoroughly before handling plants
prevention: Use certified virus-free seeds and transplants
Disinfect tools and hands regularly
Control aphid and other insect vector populations
Do not use tobacco products near plants
Plant resistant varieties where available
Tomato___Tomato_mosaic_virus.watering_advice: Normal watering schedule applies. Use drip irrigation to reduce overall leaf contact with water and soil. Clean all watering equipment regularly to prevent any pathogen spread.
Tomato___Tomato_mosaic_virus.fertilizer_recommendation: Maintain regular nutrition to keep plants vigorous. Healthy, well-nourished plants show better tolerance to virus symptoms. Avoid over-fertilization with nitrogen.

severity_tips.mild: Monitor plant closely. Isolate from other tomatoes. Control aphids.
severity_tips.moderate: Consider removing affected plants to prevent spread.
severity_tips.severe: Remove and destroy all infected plants. Disinfect entire growing area before replanting.

Tomato___healthy.description: Your tomato plant is healthy! Healthy tomatoes display lush dark green foliage, strong stems, and excellent growth. Maintaining proper care will ensure a productive harvest.
symptoms: No disease symptoms
Vibrant green leaves
Normal growth pattern
prevention: Monitor regularly for early signs of disease
Maintain proper spacing for air circulation
Rotate crops annually to different bed locations
Use mulch to prevent soil splash and retain moisture
Tomato___healthy.watering_advice: Water deeply 2–3 times per week using drip irrigation. Provide 1–2 inches of water per week. Mulch around base to retain moisture and regulate soil temperature. Reduce watering as fruit matures.
Tomato___healthy.fertilizer_recommendation: Start with balanced fertilizer at transplanting. Switch to high-phosphorus fertilizer when flowering begins. Top-dress with compost monthly. Calcium supplementation prevents blossom end rot.

severity_tips.mild: Plant is healthy. Continue current care routine.
severity_tips.moderate: N/A
severity_tips.severe: N/A
